What do you do in data? How did you get into data? My name is Benoît and I have been working for DBT Labs for four years. I worked in different teams, but now I am in the Developer Experience team. In my job, I worked a lot on the topics of AI and DBT. I worked on the skills, the engine skills that we released not long ago, the MCP server, and even on things that we also released on benchmarks, for example, between the semantic layer and the text. And also on Fusion, so on a lot of topics, but especially those that touch on how DBT changes with AI and how AI changes with DBT. So the first question is, how is 5.3? So you talk a lot about AI, they are quite central to you. What is the vision of the company in relation to this? How do you imagine the use of AI by the data teams in the next six months, which is already quite long? I think AI is a topic that is really in all discussions today, even internally. So we use a lot of AI internally at DBT Labs today. And we also think about how DBT will evolve in the world where, ultimately, most of the code will be written by agents rather than users. So we have a lot of different topics at the same time. Some of them are related to the fact of how to make agents be able to write DBT as easily as possible. Ultimately, most front-end models know how to write DBT. You can ask GPT 5.4 or Opus or Summit, they know how to write DBT. But what they know less is, for example, what is related to the process. So what is the way to think about how to create a model, let's say. So that's what we created via Skills. So via Skills, we try to explain to the model how to think before coding and which commands to launch in which order, etc. Because ultimately, doing a DBT run or a DBT build, the LLMs know how to do that. So there is this Skills side. Then on the MCP server side, if we try to make the LLM as much context as possible with as few tokens as possible. So it can be complicated sometimes. Because ultimately, when we see a lot of benchmarks that are done every day, it's often on small projects. So we see a small project, a Jeffel Shop that has 10 models or something that has 50 models. And so, generally, things work well. But on a scale, let's say, of real DBT projects, let's say our biggest internal project has several thousand models. It's really different in terms of context, with a MCP server on this type of project versus a Jeffel Shop. So there's a lot of work that we're trying to do on that to limit the number of tokens we give to an LLM while giving it all the context it needs. And so these are things that are already released. And then we're also working on things that aren't released yet, but that will come out soon. Like, for example, DBT Index. I think Christophe knows a little bit about it. It's a new scale that we're creating, which is really for agents. Users could use it. It's really something that's based on agents to make it context-oriented quickly, without using too many tokens. So it's a big token optimization, etc. So that the agent can have all the data he needs without using too much context, without doing too many loops, tools, etc. What's interesting is that it resonates a lot with one of the top articles I had in the presentation of the CloudFerq CTO, which we can jump to later. My take from the start is that the reason why MCP servers consume too many tokens is because they are poorly implemented or poorly designed. It's because you don't put the right tools in it or you don't put the right thing in it. If you have a tool in the DBT MCP server that is a return model, and there are 1,000, 1,500 or 2,000 models, it's normal that it will rot in the context window. The tool should not be getModel, or it should be getModelPaginated, or it should be getModel with a search pattern, or it should be getModel with a specific thing. And the return of the getModel should not be a JSON, because it's not efficient at all in tokens. It should be a markdown already ready, with the right thing inside. Basically, the design of the MCP server should not just be a wrapper on your API. It should be a really smart thing. And on the MCP client side, it should also be implemented in a different way. Because the MCP clients are really simple today, they just call on the thing, but it has to be implemented a little differently. And I think that all this debate, CLI versus MCP, comes back to the fact that we just have to put a little more brain effort in it for the design. And I think what you're doing with DBT Index is super interesting. I think the problem with DBT Index is the constraint of the Fusion license, for example. But DBT Index could be in an MCP server, and that would be great too, I think. After DBT Index, it is possible to generate an MCP server that has the same information. So, in fact, automatically, there is a way to expose it as an MCP server. And so, MCP clients can also use it. But your point on tokens, etc., is also something that we are seeing a lot with the DBT MCP server. I have a PR in progress, we have a tool to return the list of metrics. And so, when we do benchmarks, we define 10, 15 metrics, it works very well. Except that with DBT, we have hundreds of metrics in our semantic layer. And if I do a search on the ARA, automatically, it's already too many tokens to keep in memory. So, in fact, they dump it on the disk, and then they use GREP, they use all this to read this information. And so, it's because we also used JSON to return the data. And now, what I'm changing is making it return CSV instead of JSON. Because, in the end, we lose the possibility of doing JSON parsing, but the tokens are reduced by 70% to have the same level of information. And so, what I'm also doing is trying to limit the number of tools that you have to call. And so, instead of making five tool calls, we make API calls ourselves. We make the five API calls, and we only find one tool. And so, it's a whole optimization part of tokens that we are also looking at to make sure that we don't fill the context just with a few tool calls. Did you create a DBT benchmark? of DBT, my question is, is it still useful to write DBT tomorrow? Can't my agents just write SQL and the DBT overlay is useless? So, I saw a... I think it was our CEO who posted a link in Slack and it was an interview of the guy who created Ruby on Rails so Ruby on Rails is a framework too but even older than DBT and he said that finally, with AI Ruby on Rails is doing a little revival because agents are really good at using frameworks Ruby on Rails is a framework and DBT is also a framework and the frameworks are in the training pool so agents know how to use these frameworks and it's also relatively token efficient it uses a lot less tokens than doing it from scratch so an agent who creates a DBT project will do a few selects and then a DBT run he doesn't need to think about all the orchestration I need to launch this model first, this model after so my point of view is that agents will continue to use DBT just because it's an efficient way in terms of token to do the transformation and they already know how to do it today my vision is that in several months there will potentially be agents who will write DBT projects entirely humans won't even look at the codebase so potentially an agent will be given JSONs or CSVs and he will create a mini-project of 10 models but it's not something that exists yet completely today but I think it will happen at some point where agents will create projects from scratch the most endangered part of DBT is DBT Cloud, right? because DBT Cloud, if you have agents who do it for you who manage the collaborative part for you the product itself may have less value compared to the core, as you said yeah, definitely AI can help people maintain a DBT Core project to have your Kubernetes cluster or your Docker stack, etc. but I don't know if there's really a... it's still the difference between build and buy so if you do it yourself, you don't necessarily have support it's a bit the same with all OpenCore projects if people want support, you need to have a contract with the company and I think it's especially for the smaller teams that agents can help a lot to maintain a DBT stack for the bigger companies, I'm not sure that there will be a replacement one day towards OpenStacks because ultimately, what the commercial part of our product does is also all that is Enterprise Features have SLAs in place have things that are multi-project so it's really the platform it works for small teams but most features are also things that big companies want and that's hard to maintain yourself in a way, with agents you will create more models and DBT Cloud will help you manage your mess of more models that you won't be able to manage if you don't have the UI on top potentially but in any case the DBT Cloud platform helps when people have a lot of projects a lot of models and try to organize something that could be very messy by doing it yourself and maybe if there are more projects maybe there will be more interest to use the platform I can't predict that but I don't really see a big shift of the platform towards open source that can help OK Last thing you are going to release today I don't have a visual support but the State of Analytics Engineering you will talk about it do you have first things in mind that are interesting? different In fact tomorrow what we could have is We could have runtimes depending on who comes to your website And each person has a different runtime When he arrives on the website and each person has a sandbox On a website and the website You have an agent who lives in a runtime When I'm a visitor on a website And so the runtime He is able to do things like Access my catalog, my basket, my order history And all that is in a runtime, in a sandbox Dedicated to each user who visits my website What is the runtime exactly? It's really the front The front application And also the agent's tools, what exactly is it? For me the runtime in his case It's everything he can do That the agent understands what the user sees And the state in which the user is So if I'm on an e-commerce site And I put something in my basket The agent in his runtime has access to my basket But access to my basket is not just via calls and PIs He can instantly Understand what is in the basket Drop it in the file system Like an attached file system And then he can do his agentic loops and work on it It's like the front-end And the agent works on the same state In real time And they share the same state And so I find it super interesting To visualize it like that To tell yourself that You have to stop telling yourself We have the agent who goes back and forth with APIs What you have to be able to do Is embed the agents inside the application And that each agent basically lives As if he were the user's personal agent Who navigates in the application It's open-close in any app But agents are still Not deterministic Are we ready to put non-deterministic things In production for each user? My dream is that For each application I know my interface If I need the menu on the right If I need blue buttons That the interface is completely personalized You can imagine themes Between the applications that follow It's clearly a bit perched But I think we'll get there It's literally what he says here Each user gets A sum of different programs It's not just a recommendation engine It's not just a chatbot It's user-specific software The thing that corresponds to the software Because he lives as he goes It's an experience But I think it's really I've never seen this idea Of a sandbox like this It's not just a sandbox Of execution code, isolation What's too hard is the promotion You can decentralize And you have primitives in the middle But the interesting part is The users sharing primitives For example, you have a basket A user can have his basket He wants to share it with a colleague It becomes a bit of a mess I don't know how to handle that Each user becomes his own Product manager Adding features Removing features Exactly There's a company in France That does that That does a kind of It's quite far in my mind That does a kind of React By a user And works exactly on that We'll have to find it Somewhere in my bookmarks Maybe for the next episode

or look at it. What they do is the idea is that you can host your agents at home. They have a Hosted Service. So we come back to this mode thing where everyone does pretty much the same thing. There are some things in this article that are interesting. So what they were doing is that the problem is that you have a context in CIT, the context reset, the harness, blah, blah, blah. And so, in fact, they said to themselves, what's going on? So they virtualized the components of an agent. An agent is a session. So that's all that happened. A harness, so do you use Cloud, Cloud tools, etc. And a sandbox. And so all of this, when you're in Cloud Code, it all revolves around a single place. And so they said to themselves, you have to do an implementation where anything can be swapped without disturbing the others. And so they tried to make an architecture where basically you have something that is the harness in the middle and all the other things around it, you can change them without it being a problem. And so that's what they designed with Managed Agents. At first, they put all the components in a single container. They realized, well, it doesn't work, it doesn't scale, it's not a distributed system. And now they've moved on to something where everything can be replaced. Basically, they created something called session events that you can access. So I don't know where they explain it. Basically, in Managed Agents, you have a session. In the session, there are events. The events can be bigger than your context window. And instead of storing all of this in the sandbox or in the repel, because they said, okay, it can be in a Python repel. Well, it's a session log that is accessible with a getEvents thing. And so the brain can question the context to find out what happened in places. And that's it. The article is really good. And so you can see tomorrow, you can replace a harness with another, but keep all the stuff around it. Or test a single model on the same setup. Test several models for the same setup.
